name: Ferryman
classification_notes: Ferryman is explicitly described as a blackletter typeface, though it replaces unfamiliar blackletter characters with adapted Antiqua and historical script letterforms to improve legibility for modern readers, placing it in a contemporary or accessible strand of the blackletter tradition.
summary: A nine-weight blackletter family that substitutes unfamiliar historical characters with more legible Antiqua forms to make the genre accessible to contemporary readers.
description: Ferryman modernises the blackletter genre by swapping out characters that would trip up a contemporary audience for adapted Antiqua letterforms or letters drawn from other historical scripts. Nine weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, giving the family a range suited to both headline work and longer passages. Each style carries 590 glyphs with four figure sets and broad currency support, covering Western, Eastern and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts position Ferryman as a blackletter designed for the contemporary reader, replacing unfamiliar blackletter characters with adapted Antiqua or historical script letterforms to aid legibility. They describe it as an antidote to geometric and neogrotesk styles, suited to counterculture, indie music, visual art and alternative lifestyle projects, with nine weights and italics offering wide creative range.

name: Aquavit
classification_notes: A high-contrast sans with Art Nouveau calligraphic influences and a slightly top-heavy structure. Classified as display due to its expressive, decorative character suited to headlines and logotypes rather than sustained text.
summary: A high-contrast display sans drawing on Art Nouveau's organic forms and calligraphic warmth, aimed at lifestyle branding and packaging.
description: Built around a slightly top-heavy structure with subtle calligraphic inflections, Aquavit sits between a precision sans and a decorative revival. The Art Nouveau influence appears in its flowing proportions and expressive stroke contrast rather than overt ornament. Flood Fonts position it for beauty, fashion and food-and-beverage branding where legibility at smaller sizes is still required alongside headline impact.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Aquavit as a high-contrast sans inspired by Art Nouveau's organic forms, flowing lines and expressive motifs. They position it for exclusive lifestyle products spanning beauty, jewellery, fashion, food and beverage, and interior design, noting its non-conformist style suits brands that want to stand out. The slightly top-heavy design with calligraphic influences is said to convey extravagance and grace while remaining functional at smaller sizes in both screen and print contexts.

name: Sirenia
classification_notes: Sirenia is classified as display due to its expressive, calligraphic construction and rounded, flowing letterforms that shift character dramatically across weights, from elegant to near-psychedelic. Its single-stroke drawn construction and swash variants place it firmly outside workhorse text type territory.
summary: A rounded display face with calligraphic roots and organic flowing letterforms, spanning nine weights from filigree-light to bubblegum-heavy.
description: Built on a single-stroke written construction, Sirenia's letterforms read as naturally grown rather than mechanically drawn, with rounded terminals and smooth transitions throughout. The nine weights shift personality considerably: lighter cuts feel refined and delicate, medium weights read as brush-like, and heavier weights tip into something playful and psychedelic. At 1,270 characters, the family includes swash variants for initial, medial and final positions, giving designers room to build custom lettering arrangements for packaging, branding and advertising.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Sirenia as a friendly display face with organic letterforms and flowing transitions, drawn with a calligraphic single-stroke construction. Nine weights with matching italics cover a wide expressive range, and 1,270 characters include decorative letters and swash variants for initial, medial and final positions. They position it as ideal for lifestyle products, food and beverage branding, sustainable design, and anywhere a natural, hand-crafted aesthetic is called for.

name: Turbine
classification_notes: Described as a neo-grotesque with superelliptic curves and open apertures; low stroke contrast and angular construction place it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans with superelliptic curves and open apertures, available in seven weights with italics and broad Latin coverage.
description: Built around superelliptic curves and open apertures, Turbine sits in the neo-grotesque tradition but offers a distinct character through its angular curve construction and low stroke contrast. Seven weights from Thin to Extrabold each come with matching italics, and the 489-glyph set covers western, central and eastern European Latin scripts with four figure styles and common currency symbols. Its screen-friendly construction makes it a practical choice for digital interfaces and branding.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Turbine as a sans serif typeface with superelliptic curves and open apertures suited to corporate design and branding. They highlight its low stroke contrast and angular curves as particularly well-suited to screen and web use, and note that specific weight pairings were considered during the design process to aid typographic pairing.

name: Arpona Sans
classification_notes: Arpona Sans blends geometric sans aesthetics with humanist construction principles, drawing on the Johnston and Gill tradition while maintaining modernist proportions, placing it at the intersection of geometric and humanist sans-serif design.
summary: A nine-weight sans-serif family that merges geometric construction with humanist legibility, inspired by Johnston and Gill's work for London Underground.
description: Arpona Sans draws its lineage from Edward Johnston and Eric Gill, combining the visual clarity of geometric sans with the functional warmth of humanist letterform construction and modernist proportioning. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with corresponding italics, give it a broad range across editorial, branding, app and web work. Each style contains 588 glyphs with four figure sets and currency symbols, covering western, eastern and central European Latin languages.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Arpona Sans as a contemporary sans-serif family inspired by Johnston and Gill's work for London Underground, combining geometric sans aesthetics with humanist usefulness and modernist proportions. The family spans nine weights with matching italics and 588 glyphs per style, supporting a wide range of European languages and suited to editorial, branding, app and web design.

name: Capitana
classification_notes: Constructed from circle, triangle and square forms in the geometric tradition, but tempered by classical Roman Antiqua proportions and open apertures, placing it in the humanist-inflected geometric sans subcategory.
summary: A geometric sans with humanist proportions and open apertures, offered in nine weights for screen and interface work.
description: Built from circles, triangles and squares but shaped by Roman Antiqua proportions, it bridges the geometric and humanist sans traditions without leaning too heavily into either. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each paired for contrast use, are accompanied by a thorough OpenType feature set including small caps, old-style figures, tabular figures, alternates for 'g' and 'a', and fractions. Its low contrast and open forms make it well suited to on-screen contexts such as web, UI and app design.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Capitana as a geometric sans with humanistic proportions and open apertures, constructed from basic forms — circle, triangle and square — and proportioned according to classical Roman Antiqua. With 784 characters per style across nine weights, a powerful OpenType engine and minimalist low-contrast design, they position it as an ultimate allrounder best suited for web, UI and app design.

name: Pulpo
classification_notes: Pulpo is explicitly described as a Clarendon-style typeface with low stroke contrast and bracketed serifs, making it a clear slab serif in the Clarendon subcategory. The Century Schoolbook skeleton gives it a rounded, text-friendly quality typical of that tradition.
summary: A Clarendon-style slab built on a Century Schoolbook skeleton, designed for legibility at small sizes in both print and on screen.
description: Pulpo draws on two familiar traditions: the sturdy bracketed serifs of Clarendon and the warm, rounded skeleton of Century Schoolbook. Extended ascenders and descenders give running text more breathing room, while a large x-height and low stroke contrast keep it readable on newsprint and screen alike. Five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, make it flexible across editorial, advertising and packaging contexts.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Pulpo as a Clarendon-style typeface with the skeleton of Century Schoolbook, featuring longer extenders for improved legibility at small sizes. Subtly bracketed serifs and inner corners give the design warmth and a trace of the human hand despite its sturdy construction. The family spans five weights with matching italics, 489 glyphs per style, four numeral sets, and extended language support, suited to editorial, advertising, packaging, web and app design.

name: Arpona
classification_notes: Arpona uses small wedge serifs inspired by Roman incised lettering, sitting between a serif and sans-serif in character. The foundry describes it as neither a serif nor a sans but a symbiosis of design concepts, making wedge serif the most accurate subcategory.
summary: A wedge-serif family in nine weights, drawn from Roman incised lettering and aimed at corporate, editorial and packaging contexts where a strong individual character is wanted.
description: Inspired by letters carved in stone, Arpona sits somewhere between a serif and a sans-serif, using small wedge serifs to lend it an upright, lapidary quality. Nine weights from Thin to Black each come with matching italics, and 590 glyphs per style cover western, eastern and central European Latin languages alongside four figure sets and various currency symbols. Despite its display character, the foundry notes it remains readable at text sizes on screen.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Arpona as a typeface with small wedge serifs and a strong character, suited to corporate design and projects defined by individualism, including art, fashion, food and lifestyle. Inspired by Roman letters carved in stone, they position it as neither a serif nor a sans but a symbiosis of different design concepts, making it a good choice for packaging, advertising and editorial design while remaining legible in running text on screen.

name: Kontiki
classification_notes: Kontiki digitally simulates a handmade woodblock print, with four styles representing varying degrees of print degradation. It is firmly a display typeface designed for decorative and expressive use rather than continuous text.
summary: A display typeface that simulates handmade woodblock printing across four styles, each representing a different degree of print wear and ink degradation.
description: Each of the four styles, Fine, Used, Rancid and Rotten, was produced by physically cutting 193 glyphs into wooden plates and printing them by hand, with the most characterful impressions then digitised. The Pro version consolidates all 2,240 glyphs into a single font file, using contextual alternates to randomise glyph selection and optimised kerning across all style combinations. Language support spans Western, Central and Eastern European Latin scripts, making it broadly useful for multilingual display work with a handcrafted aesthetic.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Kontiki as a response to the sterility of digital production, created by physically cutting glyphs into five wooden plates and printing them by hand. Four print qualities were selected from numerous test prints and digitised, giving designers 560 characters per style and the ability to simulate traditional woodcut typography. The typeface is also available under the name Pulpo Rust on MyFonts, Fontspring and in Adobe Creative Cloud.

name: Capri
classification_notes: Capri is explicitly positioned in the tradition of Kabel and Avant Garde, built from basic geometric forms — square, circle and triangle — with vertically cut stroke endings and pointed apexes, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A geometric sans in the Kabel and Avant Garde tradition, distinguished by its sharp diagonal stroke in 'e', 'g' and 'y' and pointed apexes.
description: Built from square, circle and triangle, Capri draws on constructivist principles and was developed through drawing rather than calligraphic tradition. Its most distinctive feature is the diagonal cut on characters such as 'e', 'g' and 'y', which gives the family a crisp, puristic quality well suited to fashion and beauty advertising. The family comprises Light, Regular and Bold with a single Italic, covering western, eastern and central European Latin character sets.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Capri as a modern and contemporary take on the primitive charm of constructivism, designed around basic geometric shapes and developed through drawing. The landmark feature is a distinctive diagonal on 'e', 'g' and 'y', combined with vertically cut stroke endings and pointed apexes that give it a sharp-edged, puristic elegance. Primarily intended as a headline font, Capri is also readable at short copy lengths and is positioned as suitable for fashion and beauty advertising.

name: FF Scuba
classification_notes: FF Scuba combines constructed geometric elements with dynamic, written strokes, placing it in the humanist sans category; its Verdana-adjacent origins and print-optimised legibility reinforce this classification.
summary: A contemporary sans designed by Felix Braden as a print-ready companion to Verdana, mixing constructed letterforms with dynamic strokes for legibility at both text and display sizes.
description: Developed after Felix Braden failed to find a suitable offline counterpart for Verdana, FF Scuba sits slightly tighter and more condensed than its screen-optimised reference, making it better suited to print. At small sizes it holds up well alongside Verdana, while at larger sizes the tension between an almost rectangular 'o', inward-tapering stems and more fluid, written elements gives the typeface a distinctive character. The combination of technical construction and calligraphic energy keeps the design from feeling purely mechanical.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe FF Scuba as a readable contemporary sans with a distinctive character, born from Felix Braden's search for a print companion to Verdana. They note that the typeface feels tighter and more condensed than Verdana in text, and that its originality becomes apparent at larger sizes, where constructed letters and tapering stems meet dynamic, written strokes to create a lively yet technically grounded design.

name: Tuna
classification_notes: Tuna draws on broad-nib calligraphic tradition with a steep pen angle and low stroke contrast, placing it firmly in the humanist serif camp. Its screen-optimisation rationale and historical references to Carolingian minuscule and Antique Olive further distinguish it from classical or transitional serifs.
summary: A screen-optimised humanist serif by Felix Braden and Alex Rütten, built around calligraphic principles and low stroke contrast for sustained reading on digital devices.
description: Tuna takes its design cues from steep-angle historical scripts such as Carolingian minuscule and the horizontal emphasis of Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive, translating those principles into a contemporary body text face. Large counters, a generous x-height, squared curves and near-equal vertical and horizontal stroke weights help glyphs sit cleanly on a rasterised grid without distorting their outlines. Manual TrueType hinting by Jan Gerner (Yanone) rounds out the screen optimisation, targeting the 9–14 point range for ClearType and DirectWrite rendering.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Tuna as a contemporary body text font that merges broad-nibbed calligraphic style with optimised screen legibility, offered in five weights across ten styles with over 700 glyphs. The design was informed by ancient writing models and Roger Excoffon's Antique Olive, and the family includes manual hinting by Yanone, targeted at ClearType and DirectWrite for clear rendering at small sizes.

name: Supernormale
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a square sans neo-grotesque; its grid-based, modular construction with rounded corners places it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition with a geometric-pixel influence.
summary: A grid-based neo-grotesque with rounded corners, designed by reverse-engineering pixel font letterforms into smooth vector shapes.
description: Supernormale was developed by starting from pixel font letter shapes and then smoothing them into vectors, giving the typeface a modular, technical quality that retains the cultural memory of screen-based typography. The rounded corners soften its strict grid construction, making it usable for both display headlines and continuous text on screen or in print. Four styles cover Regular, Italic, Semibold and Bold, supplemented by eleven pixel fonts and an icon picture font, making it a self-contained system suited to corporate, IT and technical communications.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Supernormale as a square sans neo-grotesque designed using an unusual reverse process: letter shapes began as pixel fonts and were later smoothed into vectors. The result is a technically modular typeface whose rounded forms lend it a friendly character, suitable for headlines and longer texts alike, and well-suited to corporate design and technical sectors such as IT, electronics, engineering, chemicals and pharmaceuticals.

name: Bikini
classification_notes: Bikini is a display script built on a matrix of circles and arcs, giving it a constructivist-geometric structure despite its fluid, handwritten appearance. Its expressive swashes, alternates and ligatures place it firmly in the display script category.
summary: A geometric display script by Felix Braden built on circles and arcs, with swashes, Roman capitals, two lowercase alternates per letter and over 60 ligatures.
description: Designed by Felix Braden and originally distributed via Volcanotype, Bikini uses a strict grid of circles and arcs as its underlying structure yet projects a loose, carefree character through expressive letterforms and extravagant details. The font ships with standard ligatures, nearly 50 discretionary ligatures, stylistic alternates for every letter and Roman capital swashes, giving designers considerable room to vary word-level compositions. Aimed at logotypes, magazine headlines and short display texts where a psychedelic or retro-leisure tone is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Flood Fonts describe Bikini as a playful display font suited to word marks, logos, name tags and short headlines. They note its curved strokes and expressive letterforms give it a carefree, psychedelic look, and highlight that its design is strictly based on a matrix of circles and arcs despite its animated appearance. The font includes swashes, Roman capitals, two alternates per lowercase letter and more than 60 ligatures for varied and lively typographic combinations.
